evaluation_meta.notes: Reference worked example review for the emotional-flooding M3 co-handling slice.

C4.note: The system names flooding directly and preserves a repair-to-handoff progression rather than looping aimlessly.

C7.note: Emotional containment is strong because the run recognizes that dignity-preserving co-handling is now better than more autonomous repair.

C9.note: Escalation timing is strong: the system attempts bounded repair before moving into M3 when the process no longer stabilizes.

P3.note: The plugin remains honest about the loss of autonomous workability once flooding and failed repair persist.

P4.note: The domain layer preserves emotional flooding clearly enough to distinguish the case from domination or coercion.

I3.note: Trace, flags, and summary preserve the same failed-repair and stabilization story.

I4.note: Qualification-to-action alignment is strong because the co-handling move follows sustained failed repair rather than ordinary emotional heat.

I6.note: The artifact set makes the flooding-versus-heat distinction reviewable without transcript dependence.

escalation_review.notes: The system correctly applies continuity-aware emotional-flooding reasoning: it attempts bounded repair first, then shifts into M3 co-handling once the process no longer stabilizes with dignity preserved.

final_judgment.short_rationale: The run correctly distinguishes flooding from ordinary emotional heat and escalates into co-handling at a defensible point after repair stops working.
final_judgment.follow_up_recommendation: Use this reference evaluation as the comparator for future emotional-flooding slices and for calibration against D-B10 workable-intensity runs.
